sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Wayne, presents with a sore on his right foot that has not been healing for approximately two to three weeks.
He reports that the wound was initially a blister caused by a new pair of shoes that he purchased for travel, but it progressed to a larger wound.
He denies any trauma to the foot.
The patient had previously seen a podiatrist, but they moved and he was unable to find a replacement.
He was referred to me by his primary care physician (PCP) after finishing a course of antibiotics yesterday, which did not improve the wound.
The patient denies any pain or discomfort in the wound, but notes that it sticks to his sock when he removes it.
He also reports neuropathy in his foot.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes, with a recent HbA1c of slightly over 8.
He has a history of wearing Hoka shoes, which he purchased at REI two years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies any fever, chills, or drainage from the wound.
He reports that he has had issues with stability in his shoes.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 98.1°F, heart rate is 72, respirations are 16, and blood pressure is 110/65.
Physical exam: The patient has a 1x2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the right fifth MTP joint.
There is yellow slough present with minimal granulation tissue, but no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, no necrosis, and no odor.
There is no bony exposure on examination.
The patient has palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, but no palpable DP or PT pulses, with Doppler signs present.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with no evidence of osteomyelitis on the right foot.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; diabetic neuropathy; possible impaired circulation; diabetes; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:
I will order an ankle-brachial index (ABI) to determine the blood supply to the patient's foot.
I will perform a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue from the wound.
I will prescribe collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily and covered with a dry sterile dressing.
I will have the patient wear a surgical shoe to take pressure off the area.
I do not think any further antibiotics are needed at this time.
I will see the patient again in two weeks and may refer him to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates poor blood supply.
I will also refer the patient to a diabetic specialist to discuss ways to modify his diet and bring down his HbA1c.
Follow-up: I will see the patient again in two weeks.
